lessonTitle: Pregnancy Optimization in Sickle Cell Disease

items.html: Appreciate the maternal, fetal and pregnancy risks for pregnant people with sickle cell disease
Review prepregnancy optimization and management during pregnancy

slides.title: Pregnancy Risks in Sickle Cell Disease

data.html: Pregnancy in SCD is associated with increased pulmonary complications, infections, antepartum bleeding, incidence of pain episodes, venous thromboembolism (VTE), and hospitalization. People with SCD are also at an increased risk of pre-eclampsia and fetal growth restriction.

slides.title: Lived Experience Advocate: Ms. Thea Campbell

data.html:
Thea wanted to take the opportunity to share her experiences with being pregnant and some of the challenges she faced, which may help you when you are caring for a person wanting to conceive or a pregnant person with SCD.

Note: Thea refers to her disease as "being a carrier to Sickle Cell Anemia", but she does have Sickle Cell Disease and is not a Carrier who would have Sickle Cell Trait.

data.title: Lived Experience Advocate: Ms. Thea Campbell

slides.title: Optimizing Organ Function and Reducing Drug Exposures Before Pregnancy

data.html:
Pre-pregnancy testing to optimize organ function for people with Sickle Cell Disease includes, but is not limited to:

  • Cardiac: Echocardiogram; Cardiac MRI with T2* for cardiac iron assessment
  • Neurological: MRI brain
  • Renal: Creatinine; Assessment of proteinuria

slides.title: Maternal/Fetal Risks in Pregnancy: Preterm Preeclampsia

data.html: Maternal and fetal risks in pregnancy for women with sickle cell disease is what we will review next.

slides.title: Maternal/Fetal Risks in Pregnancy: Cardiac Iron Overload
Maternal/Fetal Risks in Pregnancy: Pulmonary Complications

data.html:
Manifestations: Restrictive lung disease, airway hyper-responsiveness, pulmonary hypertension, and acute chest syndrome

Small airway closure

FEV1 < 65 may predict risk of fetal death: OR 7.81, 95% CI 1.59–38.38, p = 0.01 (Hayfron-Benjamin, 2021)

Management: Pulse oximetry at each visit. Consider home oxygen support in setting of hypoxia.

The following can be administered chronically for pain during pregnancy:

  • Acetaminophen
  • Morphine
  • Hydromorphone
  • Chronic use of NSAIDs is discouraged at any point in pregnancy, as it is associated with fetal renal compromise and oligohydramnios. It is contraindicated after 28 weeks' gestation given risk of premature closure of the ductus arteriosus.

Neonatal abstinence syndrome (NAS)

  • Results from the abrupt discontinuation of opioids at birth after a fetus has become physically dependent
  • Opioid induced physical dependence is a transient phenomenon and treated supportively. It is important to emphasize that optimal treatment of maternal pain (often achieved only with use of opioids) is paramount to avoid ACS and severe maternal morbidity, which can in turn increase fetal risk of adverse events
  • Opioids with slow clearance rates (buprenorphine) have less severe withdrawal vs intermediate (methadone) or fast (morphine) rates
  • In a study by Volkow (2016), 131 patients with SCD were using opioids and 4% had NAS
Asymptomatic UTIs are common in pregnancy and can trigger a pain crisis. They increase the risk of pyelonephritis and in turn preterm labour. Monthly urine testing is recommended.

slides.title: Transfusions in Pregnancy in Sickle Cell Disease

data.html:
Transfusion for SCD-related complications should only be considered after discussion with haematology and careful reflection on the balance of benefits and risks such as hyperhemolysis, delayed transfusion reactions, and alloimmunization.

This chart depicts hemoglobin levels for non pregnant and pregnant patients with SCD. Note the lower hemoglobin levels in pregnant patients.

It is imperative that donor blood undergoes prophylactic red cell antigen matching for Rh (C/c, E/e) and K antigens.

Prophylactic transfusion can be considered for specific indications, after discussion with the Haematologist and MFM.

images.alt: Hemoglobin levels in pregnant vs non-pregnant patients with SCD

items.html: The destruction of both transfused and autologous RBCs
Can be immediate or delayed
Accompanied by reticulocytopenia, worsening anemia
Can induce multiorgan failure and death most likely due to damage to the underlying vasculature by released free Hb and heme
Further transfusion increases risk of recurrence!
Medications used in pregnancy for treatment include: Rituxan, Eculizumab, Erythropoietin, Toculizumab, Prednisone

slides.title: Identifying Risk for Delayed Hemolytic Transfusion Reactions (DHTR) in Pregnancy

data.html:
• Transfusion during pro-inflammatory events increases the risk of alloimmunization
  • Individuals with low number of previous transfusions or previous delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions are at high risk for DHTR — consider extended matching for Rh, K, Kidd, Duffy, and MNS
  • Jk, Ss, Fy Abs associated with a high risk of subsequent alloimmunization — extended matched advised
  • Autoantibodies, natural antibodies (anti-M, anti-Lewis) associated with low risk of subsequent immunization — CEK matched

When total hemoglobin falls below pre-transfusion levels, suggests an accompanying fall in patient's own cells, indicating hyperhemolysis. Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction in sickle cell indicated by rapid fall in HgbA cells.

items.html: Pregnancy is associated with the potential for severe maternal, fetal and pregnancy adverse events
Prepregnancy counselling is essential to reduce those risks by educating about planning a pregnancy, discontinuing potentially teratogenic medications, optimizing organ function
Multidisciplinary management once pregnancy is confirmed includes a Haematologist/MFM (or Obstetrician with expertise in SCD in pregnancy), Anaesthesia, and Paediatrics near birth
Regular maternal and frequent fetal monitoring during pregnancy is necessary to reduce risks
Transfusion during pregnancy is individualized, requires consultation with a Haematologist, and balances risks vs. benefits
